serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of kimchi is approximately 75 g, equivalent to a small bowl served alongside a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Kimchi, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169999)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for kimchi (raw, fermented napa cabbage) used as primary nutrient source. Values represent the standard fermented kimchi product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kimchi found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the low-GI profile of fermented non-starchy vegetables (cabbage GI ~10, fermented vegetables category). Glycemic load per 75 g serving = (15 × 2.4 × 0.75) / 100 = 0.27, rounded to 0.3. This is consistent with the very low carbohydrate content of kimchi and its fermented nature, which further reduces glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~498 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kimchi portion sizes to 30–50 g per meal, as the high sodium content from the fermentation and salting process can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium intake; a 75 g serving provides approximately 374 mg sodium, which is 16–25% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (2.4 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kimchi is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load; emerging evidence also suggests that the probiotic bacteria produced during fermentation may support glycemic control and insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dual profile: beneficial bioactive compounds offset by high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kimchi contains beneficial compounds including dietary fibre, vitamin C, and fermentation-derived bioactives that may support cardiovascular health, but its high sodium content warrants moderation in individuals with cardiovascular disease or those managing blood pressure as a cardiovascular risk factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi (김치) is a traditional Korean fermented vegetable dish, most commonly made from napa cabbage (baechu) salted and seasoned with a paste of Korean red pepper flakes (gochugaru), garlic, ginger, spring onion, and fermented seafood such as fish sauce or salted shrimp. The mixture is packed tightly and left to ferment at cool temperatures for days to months, developing a complex sour, spicy, umami-rich flavour with a crunchy yet slightly softened texture. Kimchi is a staple side dish (banchan) in Korean cuisine and is served alongside virtually every Korean meal. It is also used as an ingredient in dishes such as kimchi jjigae (stew), kimchi fried rice, and kimchi pancakes. Nutritionally, kimchi is very low in calories (approximately 15 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (2.4 g per 100 g), making it an excellent low-glycemic food. It provides dietary fibre (1.6 g per 100 g), vitamin C (18 mg), vitamin A, B vitamins, and beneficial lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus species) from fermentation. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~498 mg per 100 g), which requires moderation for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, kimchi is highly suitable due to its very low glycemic index (estimated ~15) and probiotic content. In Singapore, kimchi is widely available as a side dish at Korean restaurants and is also sold as a packaged condiment in supermarkets. The version served at Sukiya (a Japanese gyudon chain) is a Japanese-style kimchi side dish accompaniment.
common_user_queries: Can I eat kimchi if I have high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in kimchi?
What is the glycemic index of kimchi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169999), which provides an exact match for raw fermented kimchi. GI is estimated from the analogous fermented non-starchy vegetable category using peer-reviewed GI table data, which is the best available evidence given the absence of a direct kimchi GI measurement in the University of Sydney database.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in the input JSON was listed as 'Japanese' because kimchi was found on the menu of Sukiya (a Japanese gyudon chain in Singapore). The food itself is Korean in origin and has been correctly reclassified as Korean cuisine. 2. The input cuisine_tags ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') are restaurant menu category tags unrelated to kimchi and have been discarded. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; fermented vegetable products do not contain industrially produced trans fats. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; plant-based fermented product. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; plant-based product with no animal-derived B12 (note: some fermentation studies suggest trace B12 from bacterial synthesis but this is not confirmed in the USDA source). 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; not a significant source of vitamin D. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 0.1 from USDA source). 8. Dietary flags: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false because traditional kimchi contains fish sauce or salted shrimp; vegan kimchi variants exist but are not the standard product. 9. is_halal set to false for the same reason (fermented seafood byproducts). 10. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous fermented non-starchy vegetable data; no direct kimchi GI entry found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 11. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03311747 is the established kimchi entry. FoodEx2 code A0EJW corresponds to kimchi in the EFSA FoodEx2 classification system. SNOMED CT code 227493005 corresponds to 'Kimchi (substance)'. All codes are real and defensible. 12. Multilingual: Korean is mandatory (satisfied). Japanese included as kimchi is widely known in Japan and served at Japanese restaurants. Chinese simplified/traditional included as kimchi is widely known in Chinese-speaking communities. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not mandatory for Korean cuisine per the multilingual rules and are left null.
